# **Advanced Pole Placement and State Feedback Synthesis for the Quanser SEESAW-E and IP02 Electromechanical System**

## **1\. Introduction to the System Architecture and Dynamics**

The synthesis of robust control algorithms for underactuated, inherently unstable mechatronic systems represents a cornerstone of modern control engineering. The Quanser IP02 linear servo cart, when coupled with the SEESAW-E module, presents a classical paradigm of such a system. It combines linear cart translation with the rotational kinematics of a pivoting beam, operating strictly under the influence of gravity and a single actuated input.1 The primary engineering objective is to maintain the seesaw beam at a perfectly horizontal equilibrium while simultaneously commanding the cart to track specific linear position references, all while navigating strict physical constraints.

### **1.1 Physical Constraints and Hardware Topology**

The physical architecture of the Quanser IP02 and SEESAW-E imposes hard boundaries on the control design. The system consists of a direct-current (DC) motor driving a cart along a finite track via a rack-and-pinion mechanism. The track restricts the linear cart travel to ![][image1] mm. Mounted atop this cart is the SEESAW-E apparatus, which is a long polycarbonate beam pivoted at its center. The tilt of this beam is physically restricted to ![][image2] by mechanical safety stops.1

The sole control input to the system is the voltage applied to the DC motor, denoted as ![][image3]. The operational amplifier driving this motor saturates precisely at ![][image4] V.1 Exceeding this voltage limit results in actuator saturation, a highly non-linear phenomenon that momentarily renders the system open-loop. For an inherently unstable system, even a brief period of open-loop operation guarantees catastrophic failure, resulting in the beam colliding with its mechanical stops.

### **1.2 State-Space Formulation and Open-Loop Characteristics**

To apply modern control techniques, the continuous-time dynamics of the system must be modeled. The non-linear Euler-Lagrange equations of motion, which inherently contain trigonometric non-linearities such as ![][image5] and ![][image6] due to the gravitational torque acting on the pivoting beam, are linearized about the vertical equilibrium point (![][image7]).1 This linearization yields a standard linear time-invariant (LTI) state-space representation defined by the continuous-time matrices ![][image8] and ![][image9]:

![][image10]  
![][image11]  
The state vector ![][image12] is constructed from the available kinematic variables: ![][image13], representing the cart position, cart velocity, beam angle, and beam angular velocity, respectively.3 The control input ![][image14] is the scalar motor voltage ![][image3].

An analysis of the open-loop ![][image8] matrix eigenvalues reveals the inherent challenges of the system. The characteristic equation ![][image15] yields four open-loop poles 1:

1. An unstable pole located at approximately ![][image16] rad/s in the right half-plane (RHP). This pole represents the dominant physics of the beam falling away from the horizontal equilibrium under the acceleration of gravity.  
2. A pole at the origin (![][image17] rad/s), reflecting the integrating nature of the cart's position on a frictionless track; without input, a moving cart will theoretically drift infinitely.  
3. Two stable poles in the left half-plane (LHP) governed by the back-electromotive force (back-EMF) and the electromechanical damping inherent to the IP02's DC motor and transmission architecture.

Despite the open-loop instability, the system is mathematically proven to be fully controllable. The controllability matrix ![][image18] possesses a full rank of ![][image19], guaranteeing that all internal states can be transferred to any desired configuration in finite time via the single voltage input ![][image3].3 This property is the fundamental prerequisite for utilizing full state feedback and arbitrary pole placement algorithms.

### **1.3 Sensor Dynamics and the Dirty Derivative**

A critical reality of the physical hardware is that the state vector is not entirely directly measurable. The hardware relies on high-resolution optical encoders to measure the cart position ![][image20] and the beam angle ![][image21]. However, the continuous-time state-space controller requires instantaneous access to the velocity states ![][image22] and ![][image23].3

Because purely differentiating a discrete, quantized encoder signal yields infinite-variance noise spikes, the velocities must be estimated. In the Quanser QUARC environment, this is achieved using a filtered derivative, commonly known as a "dirty derivative." The transfer function of this filter is typically defined as:

![][image24]  
The provided system utilizes a filter time constant of ![][image25] seconds.3 While this filter effectively bounds the amplification of high-frequency quantization noise, it introduces an unavoidable phase lag into the feedback loop. At a frequency of ![][image26], the phase lag introduced by the filter is ![][image27]. As will be explored in the frequency domain analysis, this phase penalty severely restricts the maximum achievable bandwidth of the closed-loop system, dictating that the designer cannot place the closed-loop poles arbitrarily far into the left half-plane without destabilizing the physical hardware.

## **2\. Theoretical Foundations of State Feedback and Pole Placement**

### **2.1 The State Feedback Control Law**

The paradigm of full state feedback rests on the assumption that every variable within the state vector ![][image28] is available to the controller at every instant in time.6 The controller calculates the appropriate actuator effort by applying a proportional gain to each individual state, resulting in the control law:

![][image29]  
where ![][image30] is a ![][image31] row vector of feedback gains. When this control law is substituted back into the open-loop linear differential equation, the closed-loop dynamics emerge as:

![][image32]  
The dynamic stability and transient performance of the entire system are now entirely governed by the matrix ![][image33]. Specifically, the roots of the closed-loop characteristic polynomial, defined as ![][image34], represent the closed-loop eigenvalues (or poles).8 Because the pair ![][image35] is fully controllable, the designer wields the power to select the elements of the gain matrix ![][image36] such that the closed-loop poles are located at any arbitrary, physically meaningful coordinates in the complex ![][image37]\-plane.10

### **2.2 Ackermann's Formula vs. Robust Eigenvalue Assignment**

To calculate the specific numerical values of the matrix ![][image36] that achieve a desired set of closed-loop poles, the historical method of choice is Ackermann's formula.12 If the designer specifies a desired closed-loop characteristic polynomial ![][image38], Ackermann's formula computes the necessary gain vector directly using the controllability matrix ![][image39]:

![][image40]  
While mathematically sound and heavily featured in undergraduate control literature, Ackermann's formula exhibits severe numerical fragility when applied to physical systems.12 The primary vulnerability lies in the necessity of inverting the controllability matrix ![][image39]. For systems that are weakly controllable—or simply of an order greater than four—the matrix ![][image39] often becomes ill-conditioned. The determinant approaches zero, meaning the inversion process introduces massive floating-point rounding errors. Furthermore, calculating the matrix polynomial ![][image41] requires raising the state matrix ![][image8] to high powers, which further exacerbates numerical inaccuracies.10

For real-world deployment on systems like the IP02, modern computational platforms like MATLAB circumvent Ackermann's formula entirely in favor of robust eigenvalue assignment algorithms, invoked via the place() command.10 Unlike the acker() command, the place() function utilizes orthogonal projection methodologies—often based on the Kautz-Knapp algorithm—that do not require the inversion of the controllability matrix.10

More importantly, the robust algorithms utilized by place() attempt to assign the eigenvectors of the closed-loop system such that the resulting pole locations are minimally sensitive to perturbations in the ![][image8] and ![][image9] matrices.13 Because the mathematical model of the SEESAW-E system represents an idealization that ignores complex friction profiles and small variations in mass, minimizing parametric sensitivity via robust orthogonal projection is a mandatory engineering step to ensure the physical cart does not become unstable upon deployment.

### **2.3 Fundamental Limitations of the Pole Placement Paradigm**

While pole placement provides absolute authority over the exponential decay rates and resonant frequencies of the closed-loop system, it harbors several profound theoretical and practical limitations that the engineer must navigate:

1. **The Illusion of Infinite Bandwidth:** Mathematically, one could request poles at ![][image42] rad/s to achieve instantaneous settling times. Physically, the control law ![][image43] would generate feedback gains in the millions, commanding voltages that instantly violate the ![][image4] V saturation limit.15 Pole placement does not inherently account for actuator constraints.  
2. **No Inherent Robustness Guarantees:** Unlike the Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR)—an optimal control strategy that mathematically guarantees infinite upward gain margin and at least ![][image44] of phase margin—arbitrary pole placement provides zero structural guarantees regarding stability margins.16 A poorly chosen constellation of poles can result in a closed-loop system that is highly fragile, turning unstable if the track friction changes even slightly due to ambient temperature variations.  
3. **Lack of Integral Action:** The fundamental state feedback law acts as a pure proportional-derivative (PD) controller operating on the state errors. As such, it guarantees zero steady-state error only for initial condition recovery.18 If the system is subjected to a constant external disturbance—such as a slightly tilted laboratory workbench causing a constant gravitational bias on the cart—the basic pole placement controller will exhibit a steady-state positional error, as it requires a persistent state error to generate the constant compensatory voltage required to fight the disturbance.18

## **3\. Strategic Selection of Closed-Loop Pole Locations**

The most intellectually demanding aspect of state feedback design is deciding exactly where to place the four closed-loop poles in the complex ![][image37]\-plane. For a Single-Input Multiple-Output (SIMO) underactuated system like the SEESAW-E, the poles must be orchestrated to balance the conflicting requirements of rapid beam stabilization, smooth cart tracking, strict voltage limits, and the rejection of high-frequency sensor noise.20

### **3.1 Managing the Unstable Pole: The Reflection Strategy**

The system's most critical dynamic feature is the open-loop unstable pole at approximately ![][image45] rad/s.22 The physical interpretation of this mathematical entity is the exponential acceleration at which the beam falls away from the vertical axis. The controller's primary objective is to arrest this specific divergence.

A well-established heuristic in control theory for handling inherently unstable systems is the "unstable pole reflection" strategy. To stabilize an unstable pole with the absolute minimum expenditure of control energy, the closed-loop pole should be placed at the exact mirror image of the open-loop pole across the imaginary axis.23

By placing a closed-loop pole at exactly ![][image46] rad/s, the controller effectively inverts the natural falling dynamics without forcing the physical structure to move at an unnatural, highly accelerated rate. This reflection is geometrically equivalent to the optimal root locus path generated by a Linear Quadratic Regulator heavily penalized for control effort.23 Attempting to push this specific pole significantly deeper into the LHP (for example, to ![][image47] rad/s) would force the IP02 motor to generate massive, rapid torque spikes, virtually guaranteeing immediate saturation of the ![][image4] V operational amplifier.15

### **3.2 Defining the Dominant Complex Pair**

Because the stabilization of the seesaw beam represents a second-order mechanical phenomenon involving inertia and a restoring torque, its transient behavior is best dictated by a pair of complex conjugate poles. These poles are typically designed to be the "dominant" poles—those located closest to the imaginary axis—as their relatively slow exponential decay will dominate the time-domain response of the physical hardware.15

The selection of this complex pair is guided by standard second-order system parameters: the natural frequency (![][image48]) and the damping ratio (![][image49]).

* **Damping Ratio (![][image49]):** To prevent the beam from oscillating wildly around the vertical equilibrium—a behavior that would violently shake the cart back and forth—a relatively high damping ratio is required. Control engineering rules of thumb prescribe a damping ratio between ![][image50] and ![][image51].22 This critically damped range ensures a rapid rise time while bounding overshoot to less than 5%, a crucial safety margin to prevent the beam from approaching the ![][image2] physical safety stops during a transient maneuver.22  
* **Natural Frequency (![][image48]):** The natural frequency dictates the absolute speed of the response. For a system of the SEESAW's mass and inertia, an ![][image48] in the range of ![][image52] to ![][image53] rad/s is appropriate.

For instance, specifying ![][image54] and ![][image55] rad/s yields a desired dominant pole pair at ![][image56].22 This placement guarantees that the beam rights itself smoothly, with a settling time roughly approximated by ![][image57] seconds.

### **3.3 The Trade-off: Fast Poles vs. Slow Poles**

With two or three poles assigned to stabilize the beam, the remaining poles must be assigned to manage the kinematics of the IP02 cart (![][image20] and ![][image22]). The placement of these remaining poles presents a severe engineering trade-off:

* **The Penalty of Fast Poles:** If the cart poles are placed deep into the left half-plane (e.g., ![][image58] rad/s) in an attempt to achieve aggressive, tight positional tracking, the place() algorithm will compute a highly aggressive ![][image36] matrix.15 The specific danger lies in the high gains associated with the velocity states, ![][image59] and ![][image60]. Because these velocities are obtained via the dirty derivative filter (![][image61]), they are laced with high-frequency quantization noise generated by the discrete ticks of the optical encoders.3 High velocity gains will drastically amplify this noise, sending jagged, high-frequency voltage spikes to the motor. This phenomenon, known as control chatter, rapidly heats the motor windings, wears down the rack-and-pinion gearing, and easily triggers voltage saturation.26  
* **The Penalty of Slow Poles:** Conversely, if the cart poles are placed too close to the origin (e.g., ![][image62] rad/s), the cart will exhibit sluggish, wandering behavior. While this minimizes voltage exertion and ignores sensor noise, it risks failure by a different mechanism. If a disturbance pushes the beam, a sluggish cart controller may allow the IP02 cart to drift all the way to the end of the ![][image63] mm track before generating enough restorative force to return to the center, resulting in a catastrophic track-end collision.25

### **3.4 Synthesizing the Pole Constellation**

A successful deployment relies on a hybrid pole constellation. The dominant, complex conjugate pair (e.g., ![][image64]) is dedicated to the angle physics. The remaining poles, dedicated to the cart translation, should be strictly real and slightly slower than the angle poles (e.g., ![][image65] and ![][image66]).15

Using purely real poles for the cart prevents oscillatory overshoot in the cart's position, ensuring it gently glides back to the center of the track without sudden jerks that would upset the delicate balance of the beam. Furthermore, keeping all poles numerically less than ![][image67] rad/s guarantees that the high-frequency quantization noise from the encoders remains safely attenuated, preventing actuator saturation.

### **3.5 Summary Table: Pole Selection Heuristics**

| Pole Category | Target Location | Physical Justification |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Unstable Reflection** | **![][image68]** rad/s | Stabilizes the falling beam utilizing the theoretical minimum amount of actuator energy.23 |
| **Dominant Pair** | Complex, ![][image69], ![][image70] rad/s | Provides a fast, critically damped response for the beam angle, preventing destructive wobble.22 |
| **Kinematic Cart Poles** | Real, ![][image71] rad/s | Prevents oscillatory overshoot of the cart; ensures cart movements do not violently unbalance the beam.15 |
| **Avoidance Zone** | **![][image72]** rad/s | Deep LHP poles require massive gains, amplifying the ![][image73]s derivative noise and causing instant ![][image4] V saturation.26 |

## **4\. Loop Transfer Function Analysis and Frequency Domain Robustness**

While pole placement provides an elegant mathematical solution in the time domain, its robustness to unmodeled physical realities—such as variations in payload mass, ambient track friction, or time delays in the QUARC software—must be rigorously verified in the frequency domain. Because the SEESAW-E system has a single control input (![][image3]), the complex MIMO-like state space system can be flattened into a classical Single-Input Single-Output (SISO) open-loop transfer function for margin analysis.28

### **4.1 Constructing the Loop Transfer Function ![][image74]**

To analyze the stability margins, the feedback loop is conceptually "broken" at the input to the plant.31 The continuous-time plant dynamics are represented by the column vector of transfer functions ![][image75], which relates the single input voltage to the four output states. The controller is represented by the constant row vector ![][image36].

Multiplying these together yields the scalar loop transfer function ![][image74], which represents the total signal transmission around the broken feedback loop:

![][image76]  
This formulation demonstrates that full state feedback essentially creates a bespoke open-loop transfer function by taking a linear combination of the plant's internal dynamics, weighted by the specific values of the ![][image36] matrix.29 By evaluating ![][image74] across the frequency spectrum ![][image77], the designer can utilize Bode and Nyquist plots to evaluate the robustness of the chosen pole constellation.33

### **4.2 Circumventing the RHP Transmission Zero**

A notorious challenge in controlling underactuated balancing mechanisms like inverted pendulums and seesaws is the presence of a Right Half-Plane (RHP) transmission zero. If an engineer attempts to balance the SEESAW using only a classical Single-Input Single-Output (SISO) PID loop on the angle (![][image78]), they will immediately encounter this zero.34

Physically, an RHP zero manifests as non-minimum phase behavior, commonly referred to as an "inverse response." If the controller commands the beam to tilt to the right, the cart must initially accelerate rapidly to the *left*. This leftward acceleration allows the base of the pivot to slide out from under the beam's center of mass, enabling gravity to pull the top of the beam to the right. Only after this initial counter-intuitive movement can the cart reverse direction to track the beam. In the frequency domain, an RHP zero imposes a severe, fundamental limitation on the maximum achievable closed-loop bandwidth, making standard PID tuning exceptionally difficult and prone to instability.34

Full state feedback elegantly circumvents this limitation. While the invariant transmission zeros of the physical plant ![][image79] cannot be altered by feedback 14, the act of multiplying ![][image79] by the gain matrix ![][image36] creates the new loop transfer function ![][image74]. The numerator polynomial of ![][image74] is a complex blending of the numerators of all four states. By optimally assigning the closed-loop poles, the state feedback matrix ![][image36] effectively shifts the zeros of the return ratio ![][image74] entirely into the Left Half-Plane.35 This eliminates the non-minimum phase bandwidth restriction entirely, enabling the system to achieve highly stable, high-performance tracking that a standard single-loop controller could never attain.36

### **4.3 Interpretation of Gain and Phase Margins**

Once the ![][image36] matrix has shifted the loop zeros to favorable locations, the robustness of the design must be quantified using Gain Margin (GM) and Phase Margin (PM), typically extracted via the margin(L) command in MATLAB.28

* **Gain Margin (GM):** This metric, evaluated at the phase crossover frequency (where the phase of ![][image80] drops to ![][image81]), dictates how much the physical forward gain of the plant can increase or decrease before the Nyquist plot encircles the critical ![][image82] point, causing instability.28 In the SEESAW system, the forward gain is highly dependent on the motor torque constant and the amplifier efficiency. As the motor heats up during prolonged operation, its torque constant drops. A robust pole placement design must maintain a Gain Margin of at least ![][image83] dB (meaning the physical gain could theoretically double or halve without the beam falling) to safely endure these thermal variations.37  
* **Phase Margin (PM):** Evaluated at the gain crossover frequency ![][image84] (where the magnitude ![][image85] or ![][image17] dB), the Phase Margin measures the system's tolerance to unmodeled time delays.28 In this hardware architecture, pure time delay is injected by the execution time of the QUARC software and, critically, the phase lag from the dirty derivative velocity filters. A robust design mandates a Phase Margin between ![][image86] and ![][image44].39 If the Phase Margin drops below ![][image87], the physical cart will exhibit violent, ringing oscillations in response to even minor disturbances.

## **5\. Practical Hardware Tuning and Frequency Constraints**

Translating a theoretical ![][image36] matrix into a physical QUARC deployment requires navigating the strict realities of the hardware. The two most prominent barriers to successful deployment are the upper bound on crossover frequency and the hard saturation limit of the voltage amplifier.

### **5.1 The Crossover Frequency Rule of Thumb (2x to 5x)**

The selection of closed-loop poles directly influences the gain crossover frequency, ![][image84], of the loop transfer function ![][image74]. For systems possessing an open-loop unstable pole, control theory mandates a strict lower bound on bandwidth: the closed-loop system must react faster than the inherent physics of the instability.

A fundamental rule of thumb dictates that the crossover frequency must be at least 2 to 5 times larger than the frequency of the unstable pole.40 Given that the SEESAW's unstable pole sits at ![][image88] rad/s, the crossover frequency must satisfy:

![][image89]  
If the poles are chosen too slowly, ![][image84] will fall below ![][image90] rad/s. In this scenario, a physical disturbance will cause the beam to fall faster than the low-bandwidth controller can generate a restorative command, and the system will collapse.41

Conversely, the crossover frequency cannot be increased infinitely. The system relies on a dirty derivative filter ![][image61] to obtain velocity. This filter has a cutoff frequency of ![][image91] rad/s.3 As ![][image84] approaches this cutoff, the filter begins injecting massive phase lag into the feedback loop. If the crossover frequency is pushed beyond roughly ![][image92] rad/s, the phase lag from the filter will erode the Phase Margin entirely, resulting in high-frequency, destructive oscillations.42

Therefore, the practical tuning window for the SEESAW-E system is exceedingly narrow: the designer must carefully iterate the place() command until the resulting Bode plot of ![][image74] exhibits a crossover frequency strictly between ![][image90] rad/s and ![][image92] rad/s.

### **5.2 Actuator Saturation Management**

The IP02 amplifier strictly limits the motor voltage to ![][image93] Volts.1 A primary failure mode in undergraduate control laboratories is designing a highly aggressive ![][image36] matrix that looks beautiful in simulation, but instantly demands ![][image94] V when deployed. The amplifier clips the signal at ![][image83] V, the system operates open-loop, and the beam crashes.15

To handle this constraint during the design phase (rather than discovering it during a failed experiment), the maximum control effort must be calculated a priori. The control law expands to:

![][image95]  
Assuming a worst-case initial capture scenario—for instance, the beam is initially held at a ![][image96] (![][image97] rad) tilt, and the cart is offset by ![][image98] mm (![][image99] m)—the designer must manually compute ![][image100]. If ![][image101] V, the poles are too fast.

**A Practical Tuning Methodology:**

1. Do not guess the four poles arbitrarily. Utilize the Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) algorithm to find a baseline set of poles. By setting the state penalty matrix ![][image102] and heavily penalizing the control effort matrix (![][image103]), the lqr() command will generate a ![][image36] matrix that naturally respects the voltage limits.44  
2. Use the eig(A \- B\*K) command to observe exactly where LQR placed the resulting closed-loop poles.  
3. Use these LQR-derived poles as the starting point for the place() command. The designer can then iteratively nudge the angle poles slightly to the left for better disturbance rejection, or the cart poles slightly to the right to further reduce voltage consumption, checking the margins and ![][image100] after every iteration.15

## **6\. Disturbance Rejection via Augmented Integral Action**

### **6.1 The Failure of Pure State Feedback Under Disturbance**

The standard state feedback paradigm ![][image43] operates flawlessly in a perfect, simulated environment.18 However, in physical laboratories, constant external disturbances are ubiquitous. A slight tilt in the workbench introduces a constant gravitational bias force. Asymmetrical tension from the encoder cables pulls the cart slightly to one side.

Because ![][image43] acts as a multi-variable proportional-derivative (PD) controller, it can only generate a non-zero motor voltage if there is a non-zero error in the states.18 To fight a constant cable drag of ![][image104], the controller must output a steady-state voltage ![][image105]. To produce ![][image105], the cart must physically settle at an offset position, or the beam must balance at a slightly tilted angle. This steady-state error is entirely unacceptable for precision tracking.18

### **6.2 Formulating the Augmented State-Space**

To achieve zero steady-state error and perfectly reject constant disturbances, integral action must be interwoven into the state-space formulation. This cannot be achieved by simply adding an integrator block to the output; rather, the state vector itself must be augmented with a new, artificial state variable, ![][image106], representing the mathematical integral of the cart position tracking error 18:

![][image107]  
Taking the derivative yields the dynamic equation for this new state:

![][image108]  
where ![][image109] is an extraction matrix that isolates the cart position ![][image20] from the original state vector.19 The original ![][image110] continuous-time state-space system is then expanded to incorporate this differential equation, resulting in a ![][image111] augmented system 18:

![][image112]  
Defining the new augmented matrices as ![][image113] and ![][image114], the state vector becomes ![][image115].

### **6.3 Placing the Integrator Pole and Avoiding Windup**

The new control law for the augmented system is ![][image116], where ![][image117] is now a ![][image118] row vector.18 Because the system dimension has increased, the designer must utilize the place(Aa, Ba, P\_aug) command to assign *five* closed-loop poles instead of four.18

The critical engineering decision in this phase is where to place the fifth pole, which governs the speed of the integrating action. This introduces a final, severe trade-off 18:

* **Fast Integrator Pole (e.g., ![][image119] rad/s):** The integral state ![][image106] accumulates error extremely rapidly. If the cart is stuck due to friction, the voltage command ramps up almost instantly to break it free. However, if the voltage command hits the ![][image4] V limit, the physical cart cannot accelerate any faster, yet the artificial state ![][image106] continues to integrate and grow exponentially in software. This is known as *integrator windup*. When the cart finally reaches the reference, the massive accumulated integral error violently forces the cart to overshoot the target, completely destabilizing the beam.18  
* **Slow Integrator Pole (e.g., ![][image62] rad/s):** The integral state accumulates slowly. It will take several seconds to generate enough steady-state voltage to overcome a tilted table. However, it will never cause windup, and it will not interfere with the delicate, high-speed physics of the balancing beam.

For the SEESAW-E architecture, best practices dictate placing the integrator pole at a slightly slower frequency than the slowest real pole of the cart kinematics. This ensures that the primary objective—preventing the beam from falling—is handled entirely by the proportional state feedback ![][image36], while the integral gain ![][image120] acts gently in the background to slowly pull the cart back to the center of the track, rejecting unmodeled friction without threatening stability.19

### **6.4 Summary Comparison: Standard vs. Augmented Feedback**

| Feature | Standard State Feedback (u=−Kx) | Augmented Integral Feedback (u=−Ka​xa​) |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **System Order** | 4 States (![][image121]) | 5 States (Includes ![][image122]) 18 |
| **Poles Required** | 4 Poles | 5 Poles 18 |
| **Steady-State Error** | Non-zero under constant disturbance 18 | Mathematically driven to zero 18 |
| **Saturation Risk** | Bounded by initial conditions | High risk of Integrator Windup if 5th pole is too fast 47 |

## **7\. Synthesis of Recommended Literature and Resources**

To transition from the theoretical models detailed in this report to the physical deployment of C-code via QUARC, a thorough understanding of the foundational literature is required. The following resources provide the granular, textbook-quality explanations and worked examples necessary for mastery of this specific class of underactuated systems.

1. **Quanser Hardware Laboratory Guides:** The most crucial resources for physical implementation are the manufacturer-provided *SEESAW Laboratory Guide* and the *IP02 Inverted Pendulum Workbook*.1 These documents are indispensable because they provide the exact, empirical physical parameters (e.g., cart mass ![][image123], pendulum length ![][image124], equivalent viscous damping ![][image125]) required to populate the continuous-time ![][image8] and ![][image9] matrices.1 Furthermore, they provide the full, step-by-step derivation of the non-linear Euler-Lagrange equations and subsequent small-angle linearization, which is rarely found in generic textbooks.1  
2. **Aström, K.J. and Murray, R.M., "Feedback Systems: An Introduction for Scientists and Engineers":** For a deep, intuitive understanding of frequency-domain robustness, this text is unparalleled. It explicitly details the derivation of the loop transfer function ![][image126] and provides the foundational physical intuition regarding the limits of performance.33 Specifically, it explores the mathematical constraints imposed by unstable RHP poles and provides rigorous proofs for the 2-5x crossover frequency rules required to stabilize them.21  
3. **Franklin, Powell, and Emami-Naeini, "Feedback Control of Dynamic Systems":** This textbook is highly recommended for mastering the specific mathematics of pole placement. It offers a comprehensive treatment of Ackermann's formula, the numerical stability benefits of robust eigenvalue assignment techniques (such as those used in MATLAB's place() command), and clear, worked numerical examples for augmenting state-space models to include integral action (![][image106]) for robust disturbance rejection.8  
4. **University Lecture Notes on State-Space Control:** Course materials focused on multi-variable control, such as the notes provided by Purdue University (ECE380) or MIT's Feedback Control Systems (16.30/31), offer practical bridges between pure math and application.7 These resources are excellent for demonstrating how adjusting individual weighting matrices in LQR can generate optimal pole locations, which can then be manually tweaked via the place() command to successfully accommodate hard hardware constraints like the ![][image4] V saturation limit.8

3. Quanser LFJC-PEN Laboratory Guide \- made|for|science, accessed April 7, 2026, [https://www.made-for-science.com/en/quanser/?df=made-for-science-quanser-linear-flexible-joint-inverted-pendulum-coursewarestud-matlab.pdf](https://www.made-for-science.com/en/quanser/?df=made-for-science-quanser-linear-flexible-joint-inverted-pendulum-coursewarestud-matlab.pdf)

Modeling and Control of a Combined Inverted Pendulum on Beam System, accessed April 7, 2026, [https://yadda.icm.edu.pl/baztech/element/bwmeta1.element.baztech-16cc77b3-6711-4739-9302-b2ff63bd8041/c/42\_bensalah\_modeling\_9\_2024.pdf](https://yadda.icm.edu.pl/baztech/element/bwmeta1.element.baztech-16cc77b3-6711-4739-9302-b2ff63bd8041/c/42_bensalah_modeling_9_2024.pdf)
